metadata.title: Find the Pruning Cliff with Claude Code
metadata.color_semantics: teardown: INK #2A1A0E originals/axes/all text; CRIMSON #C8102E quantized/error/mash; SLATE #545454 structure/neutral
metadata.note: CLI video REQUIRED spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 OUTPUT(manim) · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)

then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes -- BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Remove 20% of a network's channels and accuracy barely moves -- under 1%. Remove 70% and the model falls off a cliff. The curve is flat, then steep, then vertical. The whole game in structured pruning is knowing where the cliff is before you step over it. Below 40% you are usually safe. Above 70% you are probably done. The dangerous zone is the 40-to-70 range where the curve looks manageable until it isn't.
beats.visual_intent: title card: pruning rate spectrum -- safe zone, warning zone, cliff zone; accuracy drop framed as the key variable

beats.narration_text: Ask for the experiment. In the terminal: claude -- write pruning_curve dot py, accuracy versus percent channels structurally pruned, zero to ninety. Shape: flat to 20%, 2-to-4% loss by 40%, 5-to-10% by 60%, collapse past 70%. Mark the 20-to-40% sweet spot and the greater-than-70% cliff.

props.command:
claude "write pruning_curve.py:
  accuracy vs % channels structurally pruned,
  0-90%. Shape: ~flat to 20%, 2-4% loss by
  40%, 5-10% by 60%, collapse past 70%.
  Mark the 20-40% sweet spot and >70% cliff."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The curve is piecewise: near-flat through 20%, steepening through 40 to 60, vertical past 70. At 80% you have lost a third of your accuracy. The sweet spot at 20-to-40 is where structured pruning earns its keep -- real latency and flash reduction with tolerable accuracy cost.

props.code:
# pruning_curve.py  --  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

# Illustrative accuracy vs pruning rate
PRUNE_RATES = [0, 20, 40, 60, 70, 80, 90]
ACCURACY    = [88, 87.5, 84, 80, 73, 55, 20]

SWEET_SPOT = (20, 40)   # % range
CLIFF_START = 70        # % threshold

for rate, acc in zip(PRUNE_RATES, ACCURACY):
    if rate <= SWEET_SPOT[1]:
        zone = "sweet-spot"
    elif rate < CLIFF_START:
        zone = "warning"
    else:
        zone = "CLIFF"
    print(f"prune={rate:2d}%  acc={acc:.1f}%  zone={zone}")
# Output: prune=0%  acc=88.0%  zone=sweet-spot
# ...    prune=70%  acc=73.0%  zone=CLIFF

beats.narration_text: Run it. The accuracy curve appears -- flat at 88% from 0 to 20%, then a gentle slope to 84% at 40%. The sweet-spot band lights up. Past 60% the slope steepens: 80% accuracy at 60% pruning. Past 70% the cliff: 73, then 55, then 20. The collapse zone in crimson.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: accuracy vs pruning rate curve; sweet-spot band (20-40%) in SLATE shade; cliff zone (>70%) in CRIMSON; curve drawn via Create(ParametricFunction)

beats.narration_text: That's structured pruning -- latency drops with accuracy. Now add the comparison that kills the argument for unstructured. Iterate: claude -- overlay unstructured pruning, same accuracy curve, but annotate that latency never moves.

props.command:
claude "overlay unstructured pruning --
  same accuracy curve, but annotate that
  latency never moves"

beats.narration_text: Re-run. Two curves now. Structured in ink -- accuracy and latency both drop. Unstructured in slate -- same accuracy shape, but the latency annotation stays flat. The accuracy curves look identical. The latency response is completely different. Embedded frameworks run dense GEMM: zeros in the weight matrix do not speed it up.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: two curves -- structured (INK) and unstructured (SLATE); latency chips STRUCTURED: latency drops (INK) and UNSTRUCTURED: latency flat (SLATE)

beats.narration_text: So: papers measure unstructured pruning because the accuracy curve looks good. Embedded hardware assumes dense GEMM, so only structured pruning buys latency. And past 70% -- structured or not -- you are in the cliff. Stay in the 20-to-40% sweet spot.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: structured vs unstructured pruning on two axes; sweet spot 20-40%; cliff at 70%

beats.narration_text: Your move: sweep your own model's pruning rate in 10% steps from 20 to 70 and plot the accuracy. Find where the curve bends. If you are hitting the cliff before latency is acceptable, switch to distillation onto a smaller architecture -- more pruning past 60% is not the answer.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) sweep 20-70% in 10% steps, 2) find the bend, 3) switch to distillation if past cliff

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
